source.title: Narcotic analgesia: fentanyl reduces the intensity but not the unpleasantness of painful tooth pulp sensations.

confidence_rationale: Double-blind RCT in Science showing pharmacological dissociation: fentanyl (mu-opioid agonist) reduces sensory intensity ratings but NOT unpleasantness ratings of tooth pulp pain. This is a clean intervention study with matched sensory and affective verbal descriptor scales, n=40, crossover design. Directly demonstrates that the sensory-discriminative and affective-motivational dimensions can be independently modulated by a pharmacological agent. Strong evidence for SD-011 separation. Caveat: the dose used may have been subthreshold for full opioid analgesia, and tooth pulp pain is a somewhat specific model. Retroactively: later work (including Price et al. 1985) refined the dose-effect relationship and found higher doses also reduce unpleasantness, which needs acknowledgment.
failure_signatures: Fentanyl reduces both intensity and unpleasantness equally (no dissociation)
Fentanyl reduces unpleasantness without reducing intensity
Descriptor scales shown to not capture the sensory/affective distinction

mapping.source_claim_statement: Intravenous fentanyl significantly reduced ratings of pain sensory intensity (using intensity verbal descriptors) without reducing ratings of unpleasantness (using affective verbal descriptors) in a double-blind crossover trial of tooth pulp stimulation in 40 subjects.
mapping.ree_translation: If fentanyl acts preferentially on the sensory-discriminative stream (z_harm_s) at the doses used, this pharmacological dissociation is strong evidence for independent neural substrates. The intensity (z_harm_s-relevant) signal is attenuated; the unpleasantness (z_harm_a-relevant) signal persists. In the REE model, this corresponds to a drug that reduces the instantaneous nociceptive signal strength in z_harm_s while leaving the accumulated homeostatic deviation in z_harm_a intact or even slightly elevated (due to the persisting afferent barrage without effective intensity attenuation).
mapping.mapping_caveat: Subsequent work suggested that at higher doses, mu-opioids do reduce unpleasantness. The dose-dependence complicates the mapping: it may be that the dissociation is a threshold effect rather than stream-specific pharmacology. Also, opioid receptors are present in both dorsal horn and supraspinal sites; the mechanism of selective intensity reduction is not fully resolved. The claim maps well to SD-011 qualitatively but the mechanistic route is uncertain.
mapping.source_context: Published in Science in 1979, this was one of the first demonstrations of the double-dimension model of pain in a pharmacological context. Gracely and Dubner had been developing matched verbal descriptor scales (the Gracely scales) as a rigorous alternative to simple numeric ratings. The paper was intended to validate the scales as well as to characterise opioid analgesia.

confidence_components.notes: Excellent source: Science, double-blind RCT, validated scales, leading pain researchers. Mapping fidelity slightly lower due to dose-response caveat (high-dose opioids reduce both dimensions) and uncertainty about which neural level the dissociation occurs at. Transfer risk moderate: tooth pulp is a specific model, and the pharmacology of the dissociation involves complexity not captured in SD-011.
